one_line_takeaway: MCPSecBench is the first specification-grounded security benchmark for the Model Context Protocol, covering 17 attacks across client/protocol/server/host surfaces, and its evaluation shows structural protocol- and host-side attacks reach 100% ASR across Claude, OpenAI, and Cursor while current defenses (MCIP, FAN) mitigate under ~30% on average.
key_finding: Every attack surface successfully compromised at least one platform. Protocol-side attacks (MCP Rebinding ATT-5, Man-in-the-Middle ATT-6) achieved a universal 100% ASR across all three platforms, and together with Schema Inconsistencies (ATT-3) and the Vulnerable Client (ATT-14) formed four structural attacks that were 100% successful everywhere and undetected by any tested defense, indicating a gap in the MCP specification itself rather than just implementations. Client-side attacks were the most resisted (Claude Desktop 33.3% avg ASR; prompt injection ATT-1 was 0% on Claude but 100% on Cursor), while server-side (all platforms >75% avg ASR) and host-side attacks (Claude 58.3%, OpenAI 75%, Cursor 81.7% avg ASR) were far more effective. The two integrated defenses were largely insufficient: average mitigation probability was 17.9% for MCIP and 28.9% for FAN, with effectiveness highly host- and vector-dependent (e.g., MCIP gave 100% RR against Data Exfiltration ATT-8 on Claude but minimal impact on OpenAI/Cursor). Claude Desktop was the most robust platform overall; Cursor was the most vulnerable. MCIP roughly doubled per-round cost (Claude $0.41->$0.76, OpenAI $0.11->$0.18) and nearly quadrupled it on Cursor ($0.11->$0.46), while FAN stayed near baseline cost.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is threefold. (1) It presents what the authors claim is the first formalization of a 'secure MCP' (Model Context Protocol), modeling the system as a 5-tuple M = <C, P, S, D, F> (clients, protocol, servers, data objects, and a sequential execution flow f1..f4) and defining four formal security specifications (client, protocol, server, host sides), where any deviation from a specification constitutes a potential attack. (2) From these specifications it derives a taxonomy of 17 distinct attack types across four attack surfaces (client, protocol, server, host), extending prior threat models by explicitly adding protocol-level and host-side threats. (3) It builds and releases MCPSecBench, a modular, extensible benchmark and 'playground' integrating adversarial prompt datasets, five reference MCP servers, intentionally vulnerable MCP clients, attack scripts (e.g., Man-in-the-Middle, MCP/DNS rebinding), a GUI test harness, and pluggable run-time protection mechanisms, used to evaluate all 17 attacks against three leading MCP hosts. This is the paper's own work, distinct from cited benchmarks (MCIP, SafeMCP, MCP Safety Audit, ETDI) which are discussed only as prior work in the Related Work section and Table 3 comparison.
threat_model: Adversaries can attack an MCP-based agent at any of four surfaces: (client) a user prompt or injected content that manipulates the LLM into generating forbidden/unintended tool calls, formalized as a violation of Process(p) => (Secure(p) AND ValidTools(p)); (protocol) a network-layer attacker who tampers with or spoofs JSON-RPC messages in transit (Man-in-the-Middle, MCP rebinding), violating Received(m) => Sent(m); (server) a malicious or vulnerable MCP server whose tools execute unverified or context-violating behavior (tool poisoning, tool shadowing, data exfiltration, rug pull, package-name squatting, command injection), violating Execute(t) AND Yields(t,r) => (Verified(t) AND NOT Violates(p,t,r)); and (host) implementation flaws or misconfigurations enabling unauthorized side effects (configuration drift, schema inconsistencies, sandbox escape, a vulnerable client with CVE-2025-6514), violating the host authorization/ValidConf constraint. Experiments were run in strictly controlled, isolated environments with researcher-provisioned accounts; no public services or third-party users were affected.
limitations: Only three commercial MCP hosts and two model versions were tested, so generalization to other hosts/models is unverified. Protocol-side attacks were shown at 100% ASR 'in controlled environments,' so real-world exploitability under production network conditions is not established. Only two dynamic protection mechanisms (MCIP, FAN) were evaluated, and only against a subset of 11 of the 17 attacks; static/pre-runtime defenses (e.g., mcp-scan, AI-Infra-Guard) were explicitly excluded from evaluation. Each attack was run only 15 times, giving coarse ASR granularity and no reported confidence intervals or significance tests. The formalization is presented as a definitional framework rather than being formally proven sound/complete, and 'attack success' depends on the authors' own violation predicates and outcome-classification, which introduces some subjectivity in distinguishing detection from common error.

datasets_used: Authors' own curated adversarial/prompt dataset targeting the 17 interactive attack vectors (part of MCPSecBench)
Five reference MCP servers (vulnerable and malicious) plus intentionally vulnerable MCP clients built for the benchmark
Real-world vulnerable client instantiating CVE-2025-6514 (used for the Vulnerable Client / host-side attack)
quantitative_results: 17 attack types across 4 attack surfaces; each attack executed 15 times per platform
Protocol-side attacks (ATT-5 MCP Rebinding, ATT-6 Man-in-the-Middle): 100% ASR on all three platforms
Four structural attacks (ATT-3 Schema Inconsistencies, ATT-5, ATT-6, ATT-14 Vulnerable Client): 100% ASR across all platforms and undetected by tested defenses
Client-side avg ASR (No Defense): Claude Desktop 33.3%, OpenAI 66.7%, Cursor 80%
Prompt Injection (ATT-1): Claude Desktop 0% ASR / 100% RR vs Cursor 100% ASR / 0% RR
Host-side avg ASR (No Defense): Claude 58.3%, OpenAI 75%, Cursor 81.7%
Server-side avg ASR (No Defense): Claude 78.1%, OpenAI 75.2%, Cursor 75.2% (all >75%)
Defense average mitigation probability: MCIP 17.9%, FAN 28.9% (over the 11 covered attacks)
MCIP achieved 100% RR against Data Exfiltration (ATT-8) on Claude Desktop but minimal impact on OpenAI/Cursor
FAN mitigated 77.8% of Sandbox Escape (ATT-16) attacks in certain configurations; MCIP negligible on ATT-16
Per-round cost: MCIP roughly doubled cost (Claude $0.41->$0.76; OpenAI $0.11->$0.18) and ~4x on Cursor ($0.11->$0.46); FAN comparable to No-Defense baseline ($0.46/$0.12/$0.11 vs $0.41/$0.10/$0.11)
Table 3 scope comparison: MCPSecBench covers 4 attack surfaces and 17 vulnerability types vs MCIP (2 surfaces, 10 types), SafeMCP/MCP Safety Audit/MCP-Artifact/ETDI (1 surface, 1-3 types each)
baselines_compared: MCIP (MCIP-Guardian) — integrated defense mechanism evaluated (Jing et al., 2025)
FAN (Firewalled Agentic Networks) — integrated defense mechanism evaluated (Abdelnabi et al., 2025)
Prior MCP security benchmarks compared for scope in Table 3: MCIP, SafeMCP, MCP Safety Audit, MCP-Artifact, ETDI
Evaluated MCP host platforms: Claude Desktop (claude-opus-4.5), OpenAI (GPT-4.1), Cursor (v2.3.29 auto)
